parsed_target.game_explanation: This game has a strong underlying gains-from-trade structure, but the players do not know each other's private values. From an analyst view, bear values BLUE most ($1.00) and RED moderately ($0.60), buffalo values BLUE strongly ($0.70) but RED very weakly ($0.10), and goose values GREEN at the common $0.50 but values both RED and BLUE only $0.10. That means goose is the natural supplier of BLUE or RED if offered GREEN at favorable rates, buffalo especially wants BLUE and is happy to give up RED, and bear can profit by converting GREEN into RED or by testing for BLUE acquisition if terms are not too costly. Because acceptance is based on each receiver's own displayed surplus, trades that ask a player to surrender GREEN for low-value chips should usually fail, while offers that give goose GREEN in return for BLUE or RED are especially likely to succeed. The main uncertainty is strategic rather than arithmetic: proposers must guess what others may value, and with two possible responders, an offer can be designed broadly enough that either might accept. Persona priors suggest buffalo is organized and fairly deal-ready, goose is also relatively willing to accept profitable trades without much holdout, and bear is patient and somewhat selective. So the likely path is early probing, then one or more efficient trades where goose gives low-value BLUE or RED for GREEN, with buffalo and bear both trying to improve their inventories under incomplete information.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start, everyone has 10 of each chip and no direct evidence yet about others' values. Round 0 should feature exploratory proposals. Buffalo is likely to notice that trading away RED is cheap for herself and may test whether someone will part with BLUE. Bear has incentive to explore trades that turn GREEN into RED, which is profitable if anyone values RED less. Goose, who values GREEN far more than RED or BLUE, is likely to make a simple acquisition offer for GREEN by giving up one of her low-value colors. Because only one accepter is realized if multiple accept, proposers may use somewhat generous offers to increase the chance of any match at all.

After round 0, the public history reveals that goose accepted GREEN-for-RED from bear and that buffalo accepted BLUE-for-GREEN from goose. Even without knowing valuations, players can infer directional willingness: goose is willing to give up BLUE or take in GREEN, and buffalo is willing to give up GREEN to obtain BLUE. This should sharpen beliefs. Buffalo now has extra BLUE and fewer GREEN, but still has strong reason to try moving low-value RED. Bear has just increased RED holdings and may continue exploiting apparent counterpart willingness to trade away BLUE or RED for GREEN. Goose likely continues converting low-value colored chips into GREEN when possible.

By the final round, the observed pattern should make GREEN look like a generally strong bargaining chip and goose should look like a likely source of RED or BLUE. Buffalo may make one last targeted attempt to exchange low-value RED for BLUE, while bear can profitably continue obtaining RED with GREEN if a counterpart is willing. Goose, having already demonstrated demand for GREEN, is likely to repeat a simple profitable offer. Endgame pressure can make proposers a bit more practical and less exploratory, favoring trades with clear receiver surplus.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This game is likely to revolve around red-for-blue exchange, because dog-blue-6080 values RED most highly ($1.00) and BLUE very little ($0.20), while butterfly-blue-3807 values BLUE highly ($0.90) and still values RED moderately ($0.60). That creates strong potential gains from trade if dog gives BLUE for RED or butterfly gives RED for BLUE. Ram-purple-9443 values BLUE least ($0.10) and RED only modestly ($0.40), so ram is comparatively less attached to either private-value color and is the most natural liquidity provider when offered GREEN or when unloading RED/BLUE at favorable terms. GREEN is common value at $0.50 for everyone, so it serves as a relatively transparent medium but does not itself create hidden informational advantage. Because each player only knows their own values during play, proposals must be made under uncertainty about counterpart valuations, but the low acceptance-threshold personas suggest players may test ambitious offers and still accept any strictly profitable deal. Dog is likely to seek RED and shed BLUE or occasionally GREEN; butterfly is likely to seek BLUE aggressively but can also buy RED if priced cheaply; ram is likely to explore multiple options and accept trades that convert low-value BLUE into GREEN or favorable amounts of RED. Across three rounds, the most likely pattern is early probing, followed by clearer exploitation of revealed willingness to trade, especially once holdings update and players can infer which colors others seem eager to move.

rounds.round_explanation: In the opening round, players have full inventories and little information about others' private values, so proposals are likely to be somewhat exploratory. Dog moves first and is likely to target RED or offer BLUE, reflecting her own strong preference ordering. Butterfly, with strong bookkeeping and low acceptance threshold, is likely to make a calibrated trade for BLUE if possible. Ram, being more search-oriented, may use the final turn of the round to test whether someone will part with RED or BLUE for GREEN at a rate favorable to him.

By round 1, some preferences are partially revealed through behavior. Dog has shown willingness to trade away BLUE for RED, and butterfly has shown willingness to spend RED for BLUE. That makes dog-butterfly bargaining more predictable. Ram has already accepted a profitable offload of RED and may continue to look for arbitrage-like trades involving GREEN or surplus BLUE. With updated holdings, proposals are likely to become more targeted and less speculative.

In the final round, there is no reason to preserve inventory for future bargaining, so players can push for the best remaining profitable trades. Dog should still want more RED if obtainable cheaply. Butterfly still wants BLUE whenever the exchange rate is favorable, but his RED stock has been reduced and he may use GREEN instead if necessary. Ram, with exploratory tendencies and lower private values on both RED and BLUE, may attempt one last opportunistic trade, though proposals that ask too much from dog are likely to be rejected because dog now has strong evidence about which trades benefit her.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This game likely revolves around mutually beneficial red-blue swaps, with green mostly serving as a low-information fallback chip rather than the main bargaining target. From an analyst view, the key private-value structure is: lion values BLUE most ($1.00) and RED moderately ($0.40), iguana values RED most ($0.80) and BLUE least ($0.40), and alligator values BLUE more than RED ($0.60 vs $0.30), with GREEN worth $0.50 to everyone. That means lion most wants to acquire BLUE, iguana most wants to acquire RED, and alligator also prefers BLUE over RED. The largest gains from trade therefore come from moving RED toward iguana and BLUE toward lion or alligator. However, players do not know each other's exact values, so proposals must be formed under uncertainty using only one's own calculator and inference from observed acceptance behavior. Because non-senders accept simultaneously and the proposer cannot choose among multiple acceptors, proposers have some incentive to post terms that at least one counterpart will take, but not necessarily terms attractive to both if inventories or expected partner fit differ. Persona priors suggest relatively low acceptance thresholds for all three players, especially lion and iguana, so small positive-surplus trades are plausible. Alligator appears deal-ready and closure-oriented, lion is patient and exploratory but not highly aggressive, and iguana is also deal-ready and tolerant of uneven splits. As a result, the most likely path is several modest accepted trades rather than repeated hard-line breakdowns. Still, uncertainty matters because alligator and lion both like BLUE, so they may hesitate to give it up, and lion is unlikely to exchange BLUE for RED at one-for-one terms despite not knowing iguana's high red value. The forecast below therefore features early probing, then accepted deals that are individually profitable on each side given holdings and likely inference.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start, all players hold 10 of each chip, so inventory constraints are loose and round 0 is mainly about testing exchange rates. Since players only know their own values, the first offers are likely to be simple and interpretable. Alligator should look to turn low-valued RED into either BLUE or GREEN. Lion should try to obtain BLUE without sacrificing too much of it, and may test whether someone will trade BLUE away for GREEN. Iguana should be motivated to turn low-valued BLUE into RED if possible. Because accept thresholds are generally low, proposals with clear positive calculator gains for the recipient have a good chance of going through.

After round 0, public trade history reveals some directional willingness: alligator was willing to part with BLUE for enough GREEN, and lion was willing to part with RED for sufficiently many BLUE. Holdings now matter more. Alligator has extra GREEN and less BLUE, lion has more BLUE and less GREEN, and iguana has more RED and less BLUE. This makes alligator especially likely to seek BLUE again, lion likely to preserve acquired BLUE unless compensated, and iguana less able to keep offering BLUE for RED. Round 1 should therefore feature more targeted proposals based on observed behavior rather than pure probing.

In the final round, there is no option value to waiting, so players should be more willing to make calculator-positive trades even if the split is not ideal. By now lion has demonstrated willingness to trade GREEN for RED when favorable, and alligator still has an incentive to unload low-valued RED for something better. Iguana remains the strongest natural holder of RED and weakest holder of BLUE, limiting some exchanges but still creating room for a final deal. Endgame bargaining should therefore shift toward practical, acceptably profitable offers rather than exploratory ones.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This game should revolve around red-blue exchange, with green mostly serving as a neutral fallback chip worth $0.50 to everyone. The strongest efficiency pattern from the analyst view is that whale-red-86 and duck-orange-4971 value RED highly at $1.00, while dolphin-orange-3122 values BLUE most at $0.80 and values RED only $0.60. That creates likely gains from trade in which dolphin gives RED away for BLUE, especially with whale, who values BLUE only $0.40 and RED at $1.00. Duck also values RED more than BLUE, but the difference is smaller than whale's, so whale is the most natural demander of RED and dolphin the most natural demander of BLUE. Because players do not know others' exact values, proposals must be made under uncertainty: a sender can compute their own surplus exactly, but can only guess whether either counterpart will accept. The three-player structure also matters because a sender cannot target one recipient if multiple players accept; this encourages proposals that are likely acceptable to only one counterpart, or at least are robust if either accepts. Persona priors suggest all three players are fairly deal-ready with low acceptance thresholds, especially dolphin and duck, so mildly favorable offers may go through rather than requiring strict equality. Whale is somewhat more exploratory and socially adaptive, so likely to test red-for-blue terms early. Dolphin is low-search and very deal-ready, so likely to make straightforward, immediately profitable proposals rather than hold out. Duck is cooperative and low-search, likely to accept clear gains and offer simple trades rather than intricate inventory management. Overall, the likely path is early probing, then one or two accepted red-blue swaps involving dolphin on one side and whale on the other, with later-round proposals adjusting to updated inventories and trying to extract remaining gains from trade.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start everyone holds 10 of each chip, so inventory constraints are loose and the main issue is discovering acceptable exchange rates. Whale is likely to open by testing for RED using BLUE, since that is her clearest private-value improvement. Because others do not know whale's strong taste for RED, an aggressive ask may fail. Dolphin then has a strong incentive to seek BLUE and can profitably part with RED at rates that would still appeal to whale or duck. Duck's turn comes after observing whether early offers reveal any willingness to trade RED for BLUE; duck likely makes a simple, gain-positive proposal rather than a complicated one.

After round 0, whale has more RED and less BLUE, dolphin has more BLUE and less RED, and duck remains unchanged. That history publicly reveals at least one accepted red-blue trade, which can make further red-for-blue bargaining more focal. Whale may now try to continue the same pattern, but with slightly less inventory flexibility on BLUE. Dolphin, having seen whale accept a previous red-for-blue swap, has stronger reason to make another such proposal. Duck, observing that dolphin seems willing to give up RED for BLUE, may also attempt to acquire RED with BLUE on her own turn, but she is competing with whale for the same source of gains.

By the final round, dolphin has repeatedly converted some RED into BLUE, while whale has already captured substantial gains from acquiring RED. Duck has also succeeded once in obtaining RED from dolphin. Final-round incentives are shaped by remaining inventories and reduced future option value: players may accept modest positive-surplus trades because there is no later chance to improve terms. Whale may still seek one more red-for-blue exchange, but dolphin's RED inventory is lower and dolphin may be more selective if the proposed rate no longer favors him enough. Dolphin, still likely to value BLUE highly, can continue making straightforward offers if he has RED left. Duck's last move is likely opportunistic, but if the obvious bilateral gains with dolphin have mostly been used, a final proposal may fail.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This game likely revolves around red-blue swaps, with green playing a limited balancing role. Analyst-side values imply turtle-yellow-8674 values BLUE most and RED almost as much, gorilla-purple-9767 values RED most and BLUE somewhat less, and zebra-blue-1097 values both private-value colors less than the others. That makes zebra the most plausible liquidity provider: zebra can profit by giving up RED or BLUE at values that are low for zebra but high for one of the others. Turtle has a strong incentive to acquire BLUE, and gorilla has a strong incentive to acquire RED. Because players only know their own values in play, proposals must be made under uncertainty about whether either counterpart will accept. The UI calculator means players should almost never knowingly accept a negative-surplus deal, so accepted trades are likely ones that clearly benefit the responder. Given the personas, turtle is relatively quick-deal and not highly patient, so turtle may open with simple, somewhat self-favoring but feasible offers. Gorilla is especially deal-ready with a very low acceptance threshold, so gorilla may both accept modest gains and post offers aimed at getting a trade done rather than maximizing every exchange rate. Zebra is conciliatory and low in aggressiveness, but because zebra privately values RED and BLUE least, zebra still has incentive to accept offers that convert one private-value color into another when the terms are favorable. The main uncertainty is that proposers do not know others' hidden values, so they may sometimes offer ratios that seem plausible publicly but are unattractive privately. A likely full-game path is early probing, then a successful gorilla-zebra red-for-blue trade once gorilla offers enough BLUE to compensate zebra for giving up RED, after which turtle continues trying to source BLUE but struggles because neither counterpart has a strong reason to part with BLUE cheaply.

rounds.round_explanation: In the first round, players are still learning what kinds of terms might clear. Since everyone starts with identical inventories, proposals are driven mostly by private values and rough beliefs about what others might accept. Turtle is likely to test for BLUE using RED, because turtle values BLUE slightly more than RED. Gorilla is likely to target RED and may discover that zebra is the most plausible counterparty. Zebra's own proposal may be mild and trade-seeking rather than aggressive.

After round 0, the public history suggests that gorilla was willing to pay generously in BLUE to get RED, and zebra was willing to part with RED. Holdings now matter: gorilla has less BLUE and more RED, zebra has more BLUE and less RED, and turtle still has the initial bundle. Turtle now has stronger reason to approach zebra for BLUE, but zebra's acceptance still depends on getting enough value in return. Gorilla may continue trying to improve composition, though now with fewer BLUE chips available. Zebra may try another simple trade, but accepted deals still require positive private surplus for at least one responder.

By the last round, there is less reason to posture because there are no future rounds left. Still, the calculator should prevent negative-surplus accepts, so only genuinely profitable offers are likely to clear. Turtle remains motivated to acquire BLUE and may make one final attempt. Gorilla now has even more RED and somewhat fewer BLUE, so gorilla's willingness to sell BLUE is limited. Zebra has shown some willingness to trade but does not value BLUE highly enough to accept weak red-for-blue offers from turtle. A plausible ending is that final proposals are straightforward but mostly fail because the terms do not line up with responders' private gains.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This game should revolve around red-blue exchange, because that is where private values differ most and gains from trade are largest. Cat values RED highly ($0.90) and BLUE moderately ($0.60), iguana values RED highly ($0.80) but BLUE poorly ($0.30), and zebra values BLUE extremely highly ($1.00) but RED poorly ($0.40). GREEN is fixed at $0.50 for everyone, so green is mostly a neutral balancing chip rather than a strong source of surplus. The clearest bilateral efficiency is that zebra should be willing to give RED to receive BLUE, while cat and iguana should be willing to give BLUE to receive RED. However, each player knows only their own values during play, so proposals must be made under uncertainty about others' willingness to accept. Because responders see their own exact gain/loss from each posted trade, offers that are individually negative should usually be rejected. The personas suggest all three are relatively deal-ready acceptors rather than punitive rejecters, so positive-surplus offers are likely to be accepted even if uneven. Cat is especially likely to try straightforward, quickly acceptable deals; iguana is disciplined and somewhat exploratory, so may test a favorable rate but still keep offers feasible; zebra is also fairly willing to deal and should recognize that giving up low-value RED in exchange for BLUE is attractive. A likely full-game path is therefore: early probing with some ambitious red-for-blue requests, then at least one or two accepted trades between zebra and one of the higher-red-valuing players, with later rounds constrained by updated inventories and by the fact that accepted trades must also remain appealing to at least one responder.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start, everyone has 10 of each chip, so inventory is unconstrained and players can test the market. Because no one knows others' values, opening offers are likely to probe whether red can be obtained cheaply in blue or green. Cat's low acceptance threshold and lower search tendency make a simple red-for-blue proposal likely. Iguana may try a slightly more self-favoring exploratory offer. Zebra, who benefits most from receiving BLUE and can cheaply part with RED, is the player most likely to post an immediately acceptable offer if earlier turns do not trade.

After zebra's successful trade, zebra now holds more BLUE and less RED, while cat holds more RED and less BLUE. Public history reveals that a generous-looking red-for-blue offer can clear, but not who values what. Cat may now be more protective of BLUE after giving some away, and zebra has fewer RED available, so exact rates matter more. Iguana still starts this round with original holdings and may infer that requesting RED with BLUE alone was too aggressive, making a more balanced offer more likely. Since responders accept based on their own surplus calculators, accepted trades should continue to be individually profitable rather than merely plausible from an outside observer's perspective.

By the final round, cat has become relatively red-heavy and blue-light, zebra has further shifted toward blue and away from red, and iguana remains a natural demander of RED if a favorable rate appears. Endgame incentives can cut both ways: players may become more willing to lock in any positive trade, but holdings now limit what can be offered. Cat is unlikely to want to sell more BLUE cheaply after prior accepted trades. Iguana may continue searching for RED, perhaps by offering GREEN as a neutral chip, but that often will not compensate responders enough. Zebra, with fewer RED remaining, may still try one last red-for-blue swap, but the terms must be good enough for a responder who now may have tighter blue inventory. This makes a final declined probing offer plausible.

parsed_target.game_explanation: The main strategic structure is driven by the private values for red and blue. Bear values RED and BLUE equally at $0.80 each, so she is roughly indifferent between those two colors and mainly dislikes giving up GREEN at $0.50 unless she gets enough high-value chips back. Peacock values RED at $0.80 and BLUE at $0.70, so he prefers red over blue and is the only player who strictly values blue less than red. Swan values BLUE at $0.80 and RED at $0.50, so she strongly prefers blue and is the only player who values red relatively little. Because green is common-value at $0.50 for everyone, it works as a neutral medium of exchange, but trading green for red or blue requires favorable rates. The largest gains from trade come from moving RED from swan toward bear or peacock, and moving BLUE from peacock toward bear or especially swan. However, players do not observe each other's values, so they must infer willingness from accept/reject behavior and public holdings. Given the personas, all three are fairly deal-ready with low acceptance thresholds, but also patient enough to test offers rather than instantly settle. That makes modestly self-favoring opening offers likely, followed by somewhat more balanced exchanges after feedback. Bear is likely to make organized, value-protecting offers and accept any clear positive-surplus trade. Peacock and swan are both relatively willing to explore, so they may try rates that could be accepted by at least one counterpart even without knowing exactly who benefits more. Since any proposal can be accepted by either of two players and multiple acceptances are randomized, proposers have some incentive to post terms that are acceptable to either counterparty, but differences in private values still matter: swan is the most natural accepter of offers that ask for RED and pay in BLUE or GREEN, while peacock is the most natural accepter of offers that ask for BLUE and pay in RED or GREEN. Over three rounds, the most likely path is early probing, then one or two trades that exploit the red-blue value asymmetry, with holdings updating to constrain later offers.

rounds.round_explanation: In the first round, players have full inventories and no behavioral feedback yet, so proposals are likely to be exploratory and somewhat ambitious. Bear knows she values red and blue equally highly and may try to convert some less strategically useful inventory into high-value chips at a favorable rate. Peacock and swan, both search-oriented and tolerant of bargaining, are likely to decline offers that are plainly negative on their calculators but remain open to deals once they see positive surplus. Because no one yet knows who values what, accepted trades in this round are most likely to be simple, individually profitable exchanges rather than finely targeted exploitation of private values.

By round 1, the public history suggests that red-for-blue swaps can clear, and players have some evidence about counterpart flexibility. Holdings are no longer symmetric, so inventory management matters more. Bear has observed others trading red and blue and may infer that straightforward green-based offers are less attractive than color-for-color exchanges. Peacock and swan, having just traded with each other, may continue testing whether they can improve further through green or by repeating mutually acceptable color swaps. Since each player sees current holdings, feasible proposals should become more calibrated, but uncertainty about private values still prevents exact targeting.

In the final round, there is no future bargaining value to preserving optionality, so players can be slightly more willing to post cleaner positive-surplus offers that are likely to clear. The earlier acceptances reveal that red can be used to obtain blue, especially from players who do not dislike parting with blue too much. Bear now has extra red and may use it or green to seek blue or hold if rates are unattractive. Peacock still benefits from obtaining red more than blue and may try one more direct swap. Swan remains the most natural demander of blue and supplier of red, but after previous trades her inventory and the counterparties' holdings constrain how much more can be done. A plausible ending is one additional accepted red-for-blue trade plus one or two declined attempts that ask for too much surplus.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This game is likely driven by a clear but privately known comparative-advantage structure. Gorilla values RED and BLUE equally at $0.30 and values GREEN at $0.50, so gorilla is the natural demander of GREEN and is relatively willing to part with RED or BLUE. Camel values BLUE very highly at $0.80, GREEN at $0.50, and RED at $0.50, so camel most wants BLUE and is roughly indifferent between holding RED and GREEN. Monkey values BLUE highest at $0.90, GREEN at $0.50, and RED lowest at $0.20, so monkey strongly wants BLUE and is the natural supplier of RED if it helps obtain BLUE or GREEN. Since all players begin with equal inventories, early proposals are likely to test whether others will accept visibly favorable terms from the responder's own perspective. The calculator makes strictly loss-making acceptances unlikely, so accepted trades should usually create gains for both proposer and recipient. The main uncertainty is informational: proposers do not know others' private values, so they must infer likely acceptance from public ranges, from the fact that GREEN is common value, and from trade history. That tends to produce exploratory offers in round 0, then more calibrated offers later. Gorilla's low acceptance threshold and low search suggest straightforward deal-seeking once a workable pattern appears. Camel's high bookkeeping and deal readiness suggest efficient, surplus-aware proposals, especially for acquiring BLUE. Monkey's strong search and bookkeeping suggest adaptive responding and selective acceptance, with a preference to preserve BLUE unless compensated well. Overall, the most likely successful trades are camel offering GREEN or RED for BLUE, and gorilla offering GREEN for RED or BLUE only when terms are favorable enough to the counterparty. Because multiple players can accept the same offer, proposers also face recipient uncertainty, which encourages offers that at least one counterpart is likely to accept without being overly generous.

rounds.round_explanation: In the opening round, players have no behavioral history yet, so proposals are likely to be probes based on each sender's own values and rough beliefs about what others might like. Gorilla opens from a position of wanting more GREEN and being fairly indifferent between RED and BLUE. But because others do not publicly know gorilla's low value for RED/BLUE, an aggressive attempt to buy GREEN cheaply is likely to fail. Camel then has a strong incentive to test for BLUE acquisition, likely by giving up RED, which camel values less than monkey values BLUE. Monkey closes the round with some information from prior responses and is likely to pursue BLUE-preserving, value-improving exchanges, often by offering RED for GREEN because monkey values RED least and GREEN more.

By round 1, players can condition on observed willingness to trade. Camel has already succeeded in obtaining BLUE from gorilla, suggesting gorilla is a practical accepter of favorable offers. Monkey has successfully moved low-value RED for GREEN, revealing some appetite for value-improving exchange but also preserving BLUE. Gorilla now likely sees that direct requests for GREEN require better pricing and may shift toward acquiring RED cheaply using GREEN, since RED is at least as valuable to camel as GREEN but substantially more valuable to monkey than to gorilla. Camel, now holding extra RED and fewer GREEN, is still motivated to seek BLUE and can target gorilla again because gorilla values BLUE only $0.30. Monkey, after improving by swapping RED into GREEN, remains reluctant to give up BLUE and may either test another RED-for-GREEN offer or try a modest BLUE bid if inventory and history make it look feasible.

In the final round, there is no future strategic value to holding out, so accepted trades are especially likely to be those that are immediately individually profitable. Camel moves first and should continue exploiting the most reliable pattern in the game: obtaining BLUE from gorilla by paying in RED, a favorable exchange for both given their private values. Gorilla, acting second, now has little reason to experiment broadly and is likely to post a simple, acceptable offer exchanging GREEN for RED if inventory allows, aimed at whichever counterpart still values GREEN at least as much as RED. Monkey closes the game and, with no tomorrow, is most likely to use remaining low-value RED to obtain GREEN again rather than surrender any BLUE. Final-round proposals therefore tend to be cleaner and more surplus-calibrated than the exploratory opening.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This game has a clear underlying gains-from-trade structure but also substantial strategic uncertainty because values are private. All three players value GREEN equally at $0.50, so green is a natural numeraire. The strongest bilateral trade opportunities are in RED and BLUE. Alligator values RED highly ($0.70) and BLUE low ($0.30), so he would like to acquire RED and give up BLUE. Otter values BLUE and GREEN equally at $0.50 but values RED low ($0.30), so she is the natural seller of RED and is relatively willing to accept either BLUE or GREEN for it. Tiger values RED fairly highly ($0.60) and BLUE very low ($0.20), so he also prefers to give up BLUE rather than RED, and he has little reason to sell RED cheaply. Because players do not know others' values, early proposals are likely to test whether RED can be bought with BLUE at favorable rates. The personas suggest low acceptance thresholds for alligator and especially otter, making them relatively deal-ready, while tiger is somewhat more selective but still not highly aggressive. That combination makes modestly favorable trades more likely than prolonged holdout. The main uncertainty is whether proposers ask for too much because they are guessing at others' private values. In particular, alligator may try to buy RED with BLUE, otter may try to turn RED into BLUE or GREEN if she infers demand, and tiger may also seek RED with BLUE but faces tougher counterpart acceptance because both likely counterparties value RED at least as much as he can credibly compensate. As holdings evolve, accepted RED-for-BLUE deals become harder if otter's RED inventory falls, so the most likely path is one or two early trades involving otter as the RED seller, followed by more failed attempts once the easy surplus is exhausted.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start everyone holds 10 of each chip, so inventory constraints are loose and players can afford to probe. The most likely opening dynamic is that alligator tests the favorable-to-him direction of trade by offering BLUE for RED, since that is his clearest private surplus opportunity. Otter is the player most likely to accept such an offer because she values RED less than BLUE. Tiger, who values RED highly, is likely to decline. Otter then has a chance to exploit any inferred demand for RED by asking for BLUE in return. Tiger's opening move is more uncertain: he also wants to exchange BLUE away for RED, but after observing earlier turns he may temper his ask if otter has already sold some RED.

After round 0, otter has already converted some low-value RED into BLUE, so she remains the most plausible RED supplier but has less inventory slack and may ask slightly better terms. Alligator has successfully acquired RED and may try again, but now he has seen that otter is willing to trade, so he may push for a slightly more favorable ratio. Tiger has also bought RED once, which may make him willing to keep searching, but his counterpart options remain limited because alligator is not a natural RED seller. With some gains already realized, round 1 is likely to feature a mix of one successful otter-centered exchange and at least one rejected overreach.

By the final round, the easy RED-for-BLUE surplus has mostly been harvested. Otter now holds relatively less RED and more BLUE, so further sales of RED become less compelling unless compensated well. Alligator still values RED the most and may make one more acceptable offer, but he is also low-search and deal-ready, so he may settle for a straightforward exchange rather than a complicated gamble. Tiger, with low search and shorter horizon, may still try one last simple proposal, but unless he offers enough, the remaining counterparties have little reason to part with RED. Final-round behavior is therefore likely to produce one last accepted alligator-otter trade and a final tiger rejection.

Persona priors suggest gorilla is relatively conciliatory but moderately cautious, so likely to make simple, visibly fair-looking offers. Llama is deal-ready and less guarded, so likely to accept modest gains and make practical offers quickly. Whale is also deal-ready, conciliatory, and somewhat better at bookkeeping, so likely to spot profitable trades and accept positive-surplus offers readily. Across the full game, the likely pattern is that gorilla's BLUE becomes the main target of profitable exchanges, while llama and whale may have trouble trading with each other because their valuations are identical and thus there is little private-value wedge between them except through GREEN-based inventory management. rounds.round_explanation: In the first round, players are still learning from behavior rather than from direct value revelation. parsed_target.game_explanation: This game has a strong asymmetry in private values that should shape bargaining even though players do not observe one another's exact payoffs. That makes tiger the likely demander of blue and red and the natural supplier of green if tiger can get favorable terms. Duck values RED and GREEN equally at $0.50 and values BLUE poorly at $0.20, so duck is the clearest seller of blue and is relatively flexible between red and green. Lion is intermediate, valuing RED at $0.40, BLUE at $0.30, and GREEN at $0.50, so lion tends to prefer getting green and is somewhat willing to give up red or blue if the rate is good enough. Because accept/reject decisions are based on each receiver's own surplus calculator, any accepted trade must be privately profitable for the realized recipient. This creates friction: proposers may try ambitious offers, but counterparties can instantly see if they lose value and reject. The three-player structure adds uncertainty because a sender cannot target a specific partner; if multiple players would profitably accept, the recipient is random. Given the personas, duck is likely to make relatively straightforward, low-conflict offers and accept modest gains, lion is more guarded and somewhat selective, and tiger is likely to recognize profitable exchanges but not search endlessly. A likely full-game path is that duck successfully trades away low-value BLUE for GREEN, lion experiments with a green-for-red request that duck can profitably accept, and tiger then exploits high own values by offering GREEN for RED or BLUE at rates that are attractive to at least one lower-value holder. As inventories update, later-round proposals become more constrained, and some final-turn offers may fail because the remaining profitable counterparties are fewer or because proposers reach for slightly too much surplus.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start all players hold 10 of each chip, so inventory constraints are loose and the main force is value asymmetry. By the final round, duck has shed much of the low-value BLUE and gained valuable GREEN, lion still prefers GREEN but may find counterparties less willing at lion's desired rates, and tiger has accumulated additional high-value RED and BLUE. parsed_target.game_explanation: This game has a clear but privately hidden comparative-advantage structure. rounds.round_explanation: At the start, all players hold 10 of each color. The strongest hidden gains come from exchanging RED and BLUE, but no one knows that directly. rounds.round_explanation: In the opening round, players are still learning through revealed behavior rather than knowing each other's values. Dog is the most likely to test the obvious comparative-advantage trade by offering blue for red, since blue is relatively cheap for dog and valuable to both opponents. Parrot and turtle are each likely to accept only if the posted calculator shows an immediate gain, and because both value blue very highly, a moderate red-for-blue trade can attract both. Parrot's own proposal is likely to seek more blue, but dog may not accept if the terms are not clearly profitable. parsed_target.game_explanation: This game has a clear hidden-gains structure but also substantial strategic uncertainty because values are private. Because players know only their own values during play, each proposer must guess what kinds of offers others will accept. The acceptance UI makes players unlikely to accept negative-surplus offers, so successful trades should usually be individually profitable for both sides. Persona priors suggest badger is relatively deal-ready and low-search, so she may make straightforward, acceptance-oriented offers rather than prolonged experimentation. Peacock is disciplined and less aggressive, so he is likely to construct feasible, measured offers and may use green or blue to try to obtain red. Overall, the likely path is that early rounds test counterpart willingness with simple red-for-blue or red-for-green proposals, and at least one trade involving peacock acquiring red or unicorn acquiring blue is especially plausible. rounds.round_explanation: In the opening round, everyone starts with symmetric inventories, so proposals are driven almost entirely by private values and rough beliefs about others. Badger likely tries a simple exchange to improve her blue holdings, but because she does not know who values her offered chip enough, an opening offer may be too optimistic and get declined. Peacock, with strong bookkeeping and low aggressiveness, is likely to make a balanced-looking offer for red or to leverage his low value for blue. By round 1, players have seen which broad directions of trade are acceptable. Unicorn has also shown interest in acquiring blue by giving up red. Badger, being more deal-ready than strategic-exploratory, is likely to respond with a more conservative offer than her opening attempt. With inventories now changed, proposals must also respect updated chip constraints, and green becomes more useful as a low-conflict medium when direct red-blue rates are hard to agree on.

In the final round, players have limited remaining chances, so proposals often become more acceptance-oriented. rounds.round_explanation: At the start everyone has 10 of each chip, so inventory constraints are loose and players can test the market. In the final round there is no future value to experimentation, so proposals should become more directly payoff-focused. parsed_target.game_explanation: This game is likely to revolve around red-for-blue exchange, with green mostly serving as a neutral fallback chip rather than the core of bargaining. Alligator and buffalo have identical private values: RED=$1.00, BLUE=$0.70, GREEN=$0.50. Rabbit values the same ranking but slightly lower: RED=$0.90, BLUE=$0.60, GREEN=$0.50. So every player most wants red, then blue, then green. Because rankings are aligned, many seemingly simple trades are unattractive on both sides: nobody wants to give up red cheaply, and green is too low-value to buy much red unless offered in large quantities. The key zone for mutually acceptable trade is when someone gives blue to get red at a rate that is favorable under their own values and still acceptable to the counterparty's own calculator. For example, 2 RED for 3 BLUE is profitable for the proposer if they value red highly enough, and for rabbit it can be acceptable to give 2 RED for 3 BLUE because 3 BLUE is worth $1.80 to rabbit versus $1.80 lost in red, making it weakly acceptable. However, for alligator or buffalo, 2 RED for 3 BLUE is not acceptable because they lose $2.00 in red for only $2.10 in blue only if they are the seller; they would accept only if the direction benefits them. Since responders can see their exact own gain or loss, proposals should cluster near individually profitable frontiers rather than rely on persuasion. Persona priors suggest alligator and rabbit are both relatively deal-ready with very low acceptance thresholds and strong bookkeeping, so they should accept any nonnegative trade quickly. Buffalo is more conciliatory than aggressive but somewhat more selective and anchor-sensitive, so buffalo may post moderate rather than extreme offers and may accept deals that look straightforward and not exploitative. The main uncertainty comes from three-player competition: when a proposal is profitable to both non-senders, both may accept, and the recipient is then randomly chosen. That makes proposals slightly riskier to target precisely, and it can induce proposers to use terms likely to attract at least one acceptance rather than optimize for a specific partner. Because holdings update after each trade and inventories begin symmetric, a plausible path is that early modest red-for-blue deals execute, gradually moving red toward alligator and buffalo when rabbit is willing to part with some red for enough blue, while later rounds become more constrained as rabbit's red inventory falls and blue inventories shift.

rounds.round_explanation: In the opening round, players are still learning from public behavior because private values are unknown to others. That encourages offers that are self-serving but still plausibly acceptable. Alligator, with high bookkeeping and very low acceptance threshold, is likely to test a profitable red-for-blue trade first. Buffalo, being less aggressive, may make a balanced-looking offer if alligator's succeeds or a slightly softer one if it fails. Rabbit is exploratory and deal-ready, but because rabbit values red a bit less than the others, rabbit is the most natural early source of red in exchange for blue.

By round 1, the history reveals that rabbit was willing to exchange red for blue at the 2-for-3 rate, while green-for-blue did not clear. That should strengthen expectations that rabbit is the likely counterparty for more red-for-blue trades. At the same time, rabbit's red inventory is now lower, so later offers may either become smaller or shift to blue-versus-green balancing attempts. Proposers can also infer from rejections that green is not an attractive payment for blue at rough parity.

In the final round, inventories and revealed willingness matter most because there is no future opportunity value from waiting. Rabbit has likely become relatively blue-heavy and red-light, while alligator and buffalo are red-heavier. That makes further rabbit-to-others red sales harder simply because rabbit has less red left. Final-round proposals are therefore likely to become either smaller opportunistic red-for-blue trades or unsuccessful attempts to rebalance using green. Since responders accept based on direct own surplus, any proposal that is negative even by a little should still be rejected despite the endgame.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This game should center on red-for-blue exchange, because red is most valuable to zebra-blue-1097 ($0.80) and least valuable to turtle-yellow-8674 ($0.10), while blue is most valuable to turtle-yellow-8674 ($0.70) and relatively low for zebra-blue-1097 ($0.30). That creates the clearest gains from trade between those two. Gorilla-purple-9767 sits in the middle, liking blue ($0.40) more than red ($0.20), and valuing green at the fixed common $0.50 like everyone else. Because players only know their own values during play, they cannot directly identify the efficient counterpart; instead they infer from proposals and acceptances. The interface shows exact own-surplus consequences, so accept decisions should be disciplined by whether the deal is positive for the responder. Zebra's low aggressiveness and low acceptance threshold make her likely to open with feasible, deal-seeking offers for blue in exchange for red, even if she cannot perfectly price them. Gorilla is also deal-ready and exploratory, so she may test blue-for-red requests or green-for-red requests, but she faces uncertainty because either opponent could accept and the recipient is random if both do. Turtle is quick-deal oriented and should recognize that giving up red for blue can be very profitable, but may avoid over-searching and settle for plainly positive offers. Across the full game, accepted trades are most likely when zebra offers red for blue at terms profitable to turtle, or when turtle requests red while paying blue. Green is a weaker bargaining instrument because it has identical value for all players, so it only works when paired against someone else's lower-valued color. The main uncertainty is that proposers do not know others' private values, so some ambitious offers that look good to the sender will still be declined, especially if they ask too much blue for too little red.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start all players hold 10 of each color, so inventory does not yet constrain proposals. Early bargaining should function as price discovery. Zebra is the most natural candidate to test the red-for-blue channel first, because that is her strongest private opportunity. Gorilla may probe for red as well, but without knowing who values red least. Turtle is likely to accept only clearly favorable trades and may wait for an obvious bargain rather than engage in complicated experimentation.

After round 0, zebra has already improved by converting some red into blue and then keeping red away from a costly trade, while gorilla has learned that asking for red too cheaply is unlikely to work. Turtle has also learned that at least one counterpart will hand over red for blue at favorable terms. The second round should feature somewhat sharper targeting: zebra can continue exploiting her low value for blue, gorilla may turn toward green as a less identity-revealing medium, and turtle may continue simple, positive red-for-blue requests.

By the final round, the public history suggests that red-for-blue trades can succeed but only at terms favorable enough for the responder. Zebra has the strongest incentive to keep trading red away for blue, and turtle remains the likely partner because red is nearly worthless to her and blue is highly valuable. Gorilla is still flexible but lacks a comparably strong private arbitrage opportunity, so her proposals are more speculative and easier to reject. Final-round urgency may make zebra and turtle slightly more willing to close on straightforward positive-surplus deals.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This game should revolve around red-blue exchange, not green. Green is worth $0.50 to everyone, so it is a neutral inventory item and not a source of comparative advantage. The private-value gains come from red and blue: cat-green-4185 values BLUE most ($0.70) and RED/GREEN equally ($0.50), iguana-yellow-3262 values RED most ($0.80) and BLUE least ($0.30), and zebra-pink-2457 values BLUE most ($0.80) and RED least ($0.40). That means cat and zebra both want blue, while iguana strongly wants red and is the natural supplier of blue in exchange for red. The highest-surplus bilateral direction is therefore usually iguana giving BLUE to cat or zebra in return for RED. Cat can also profitably give RED for BLUE, though less aggressively than zebra from the outside observer's perspective. Because players only know their own values during play, proposers cannot perfectly target the best counterpart; they must post a public offer that either non-sender can accept, and if both accept the recipient is randomized. That creates uncertainty, especially for offers that are attractive to both opponents. Persona priors suggest all three are relatively deal-ready with low acceptance thresholds, so modest positive-surplus offers are likely to be accepted rather than rejected on principle. Cat is especially likely to try straightforward, implementable trades and accept thin gains. Iguana is disciplined and somewhat exploratory, so she may test whether others will part with red at favorable rates. Zebra is also deal-ready but somewhat less precise in bookkeeping, so she may make simple value-seeking offers for blue or accept clearly positive deals. Overall, the likely pattern is early probing with possibly one failed ambitious offer, followed by multiple accepted red-for-blue exchanges, with acceptance driven by each responder's on-screen surplus calculator rather than fairness concerns.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start all players hold 10 of each chip, so there are no inventory constraints yet. The main strategic question is whether proposers ask for favorable red-blue ratios or start with safer, likely acceptable terms. Since cat knows only that blue is personally valuable and red is less so, cat is likely to propose giving RED for BLUE. Iguana, who privately values blue very little, is the most likely accepter of such an offer, while zebra is unlikely to give up blue cheaply because blue is her best chip. Iguana's own proposal should then push in the opposite direction: she wants RED and can afford to give BLUE. Zebra, seeing blue as highly valuable and red as weak, may also attempt to trade away RED for BLUE when her turn arrives. Because all three are fairly willing to deal, round 0 likely establishes the market direction quickly.

By round 1, cat has already improved by swapping some red for blue, iguana has increased red and reduced blue, and zebra has also turned some blue into red and may now want to reverse part of that if possible. Public history reveals that red-for-blue trades are getting accepted, so proposers can calibrate better. Cat is likely to repeat a moderate red-for-blue request because it worked before. Iguana, after seeing demand for blue from others, may try to extract even more red per blue while she still has a blue surplus from her own perspective. Zebra may avoid another overly aggressive ask if her earlier attempt failed and instead post a more balanced offer that can tempt cat, who still values blue above red but may accept if the deal is only mildly costly or slightly beneficial depending on quantities.

In the final round, inventories are more uneven and players can infer some counterpart tendencies from the public record, though not private values. Iguana has repeatedly shown willingness to give blue for red, which may make cat and zebra continue targeting her. Since there is no future after this round, proposers may become slightly more opportunistic, but low acceptance thresholds still favor deals with clear own-side gains. Cat likely sticks with the simple conversion of RED into BLUE. Iguana may continue seeking RED, but with a shrinking blue inventory she may either ask for a steeper exchange or use GREEN as a secondary payment chip if she wants to preserve blue. Zebra, in the final turn, is likely to make one last straightforward attempt to gain BLUE with RED at a rate that can still attract iguana. The most likely ending is another accepted red-for-blue trade rather than a complex green-based exchange.
