parsed_target.game_explanation: This game likely revolves around red-for-blue exchange, with green mostly serving as a low-information fallback chip rather than the main bargaining target. Using the analyst-side valuations, bear values BLUE most ($1.00) and also likes RED ($0.70), so bear most wants to acquire blue and is willing to part with red or green. Swan also values BLUE highly ($0.90), but values RED only at $0.50, so swan is the most natural seller of blue if offered enough red. Peacock values RED ($0.60) more than BLUE ($0.40), making peacock another plausible blue seller and red buyer. Because green is worth the same $0.50 to everyone, green-for-anything trades need quantity terms that clearly compensate the other side; otherwise they are easy to reject. A key friction is that players do not know each other's exact values, so a proposer must guess what another player might accept. The low acceptance-threshold personas suggest all three are fairly willing to accept positive-surplus offers, especially peacock and swan, but search-oriented and patient tendencies imply they may still test slightly ambitious terms before settling. Since multiple acceptances are randomly assigned, proposers also face some uncertainty about which accepting player becomes the counterparty, so they may prefer offers that are acceptable to only one likely recipient. Overall, the most likely path is some early probing, followed by mutually beneficial red-for-blue trades, especially involving bear trying to convert red into blue and peacock trying to convert blue into red.

rounds.round_explanation: In the opening round, players are still inferring counterpart willingness. Bear moves first and is the player with the strongest private incentive to buy blue. A cautious but deal-ready opening is likely to ask for blue while offering red at a rate that is profitable for bear and plausibly acceptable to at least one blue-holder. Peacock then has incentives to seek red by selling blue, since red is more valuable than blue to peacock. Swan, moving last, can condition on the first two outcomes and may try a straightforward blue-for-red trade if inventory still permits.

By round 1, trade history reveals that red-for-blue offers can clear, and inventories begin to matter. Bear has already increased blue holdings and reduced red, so bear may either continue buying blue more selectively or try using green as a lower-cost offer, though green-based proposals are harder to place because green has only moderate common value. Peacock, after one accepted incoming trade and one failed proposal, likely continues targeting red if possible but may need to sweeten terms. Swan has seen that bear is a willing blue buyer and may again try to exchange blue for red if bear still has enough red remaining.

In the final round, inventories are more uneven and there is less reason to hold out for future opportunities. Bear now has relatively little red left and substantial blue, making another red-for-blue purchase harder unless terms are small. Peacock is likely to continue looking for profitable conversions into red but may be constrained by remaining blue. Swan, having repeatedly benefited from selling blue for red, may continue doing so if bear still has red available. Endgame behavior is therefore likely to feature one more small feasible red-for-blue deal plus at least one declined proposal that is profitable for the proposer but no longer attractive enough for the available counterparties.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This game has strong gains from trade because the players' private values are sharply misaligned on red and blue chips while green is fixed at $0.50 for everyone. Gorilla values RED most highly ($1.00) and BLUE very little ($0.10), so gorilla most wants to convert blue into red and strongly resists giving up red. Camel values BLUE relatively highly ($0.60) and RED relatively poorly ($0.30), so camel is the natural seller of red and buyer of blue. Monkey values both RED ($1.00) and BLUE ($0.90) highly, so monkey is generally reluctant to part with either and has less reason than the others to trade unless the terms are clearly favorable. From an analyst view, the easiest mutually beneficial bilateral trade is camel giving RED for BLUE: camel gains because blue is worth more than red to him, and either gorilla or monkey may accept depending on the ratio. The main uncertainty is that players do not know each other's valuations, only their own. That means gorilla may test ambitious offers for red using blue, camel may experiment with offers that exploit likely demand for red, and monkey may be selective because his inventory is already valuable to him. The personas suggest gorilla is patient but not highly exploratory, so likely to make simple, surplus-positive offers from her own perspective and accept decent deals. Camel is disciplined and deal-ready, so likely to identify feasible value-improving exchanges and post calibrated proposals. Monkey is search-oriented and disciplined, so likely to reject weak deals, preserve high-value inventory, and only accept offers with clear personal gain. Overall, the forecast is a game with one or two successful RED-for-BLUE trades centered on camel, plus several rejected attempts where gorilla or monkey ask for red too cheaply or try to unload low-value blue at terms that are unattractive to likely counterparties.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start all three players hold 10 of each chip. Early in the game, players are likely to probe for obvious gains without yet having much evidence about others' willingness to trade. Gorilla's private incentives point toward asking for RED while offering BLUE, since that is extremely profitable if accepted. Camel is best positioned to make the most plausible accepted trade because he is the one player who privately benefits from giving up RED in exchange for BLUE. Monkey, who values both RED and BLUE highly, is likely to be choosy in round 0 and may decline offers that would be attractive to other types but not to him.

After round 0, gorilla has more RED and less BLUE, camel has more BLUE and less RED, and monkey is unchanged. The public history now reveals that a RED-for-BLUE trade can clear, which may encourage further attempts in that direction. Gorilla, now holding even more of her favorite chip, still has strong incentive to seek additional RED but has fewer blue chips to use and may continue to anchor on ambitious terms. Camel has shown willingness to convert RED into BLUE and may continue doing so while his inventory allows. Monkey still has little reason to surrender RED or BLUE cheaply, so he remains the most selective responder.

By the final round, camel has further shifted toward BLUE and has reduced RED inventory, while gorilla has accumulated RED and run down BLUE. Endgame behavior often becomes less exploratory and more opportunistic: senders may post a final favorable offer if they see a feasible counterpart, but weak terms are still likely to be rejected because there is no strategic reason for responders to accept negative private surplus. Camel may still squeeze one more profitable RED-for-BLUE trade if he has enough red left, but monkey remains unlikely to sell high-value chips. Gorilla's remaining proposals are constrained by her lower blue stock and strong reluctance to part with red.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This game has a clear surplus pattern but also meaningful strategic uncertainty because values are private during play. Alligator values BLUE most ($0.60) and RED least ($0.10), so alligator most wants to turn RED into BLUE or GREEN. Otter values BLUE extremely highly ($1.00), values RED moderately ($0.40), and is indifferent on GREEN at $0.50, so otter strongly dislikes giving up BLUE and will often prefer acquiring BLUE or unloading GREEN for useful chips. Tiger values RED highly ($0.70) and BLUE highly ($0.90), with GREEN lowest at $0.50, so tiger is a natural buyer of RED and seller of GREEN when terms are favorable. The main efficient direction is therefore from alligator's excess RED toward tiger, often paid with GREEN, and sometimes with BLUE if tiger experiments. A second useful direction is GREEN from otter toward tiger in exchange for RED, since tiger likes RED more than GREEN while otter does not value RED much. However, because proposers do not know others' exact values and because any accepting non-sender can be randomly selected if multiple players accept, proposers have an incentive to post deals that are attractive enough to at least one likely counterparty but not too generous. Persona priors suggest alligator and otter are relatively deal-ready accepters, while tiger is somewhat more selective but disciplined. That points to some early exploratory but simple offers, moderate acceptance of clearly profitable trades, and limited bluffing or long multi-round holdout. Holdings also matter: after each accepted trade, the feasible and attractive offers shift, especially as some players accumulate the chip types they most value and run down the types they least value.

rounds.round_explanation: Round 0 is likely to feature exploratory offers built from each player's own visible calculator and rough beliefs about what others may value. With no trade history yet, proposers must infer counterpart demand from the public chip ranges and common bargaining logic. Alligator is the clearest candidate to test a RED-for-GREEN trade because that is strongly profitable for alligator and plausibly attractive to someone who values RED more than GREEN. Otter, who values BLUE most and has a very low acceptance threshold, is likely to pursue a practical trade that converts GREEN into RED or tests whether someone will part with BLUE. Tiger, being more state-disciplined and holding high values for both RED and BLUE, is likely to avoid giving those away cheaply and instead may seek BLUE using GREEN if prior turns do not already improve his position.

By Round 1, the first two accepted trades reveal some structure. Tiger has already shown willingness to give GREEN for RED, making it more likely others see tiger as a red buyer rather than an easy source of BLUE. Otter has also demonstrated openness to using GREEN as payment. Alligator, after improving by unloading RED, is still likely to want further conversion of low-value RED into something better, though the easiest gains may now be smaller. Otter still values BLUE so highly that giving it away remains unlikely unless the terms are very strong. Tiger, having failed to buy BLUE in the previous round, may continue to probe but should be somewhat cautious about overpaying in high-value RED.

In the final round, players no longer need to preserve optionality for later turns, so they can make more direct value-improving proposals. Alligator now has even fewer reasons to hold RED and may again try to convert residual RED into GREEN. Otter still strongly prefers holding BLUE and will likely continue using GREEN as the main currency for acquisitions, especially RED when available at favorable terms. Tiger has finally managed to obtain some BLUE, reducing urgency, and may become less willing to part with either RED or BLUE unless the exchange is clearly profitable. Final-round behavior should therefore feature one more alligator-to-tiger red-for-green trade and a possibly overambitious tiger offer that gets declined.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This game is likely to revolve around red chips. Dolphin values RED and BLUE at only $0.10 each and GREEN at $0.50, so dolphin strongly wants to turn low-value red or blue into green if possible. Frog values RED at $0.90 and BLUE/GREEN at $0.50, so frog is the clear high-value red buyer and should be willing to part with green or blue to acquire red. Pig values RED at $0.40, BLUE at $0.30, and GREEN at $0.50, so pig mildly prefers green most, but can profit by accepting favorable red-for-blue or red-for-green offers depending on direction. Because players only know their own values during play, there is uncertainty about who values what, but simple acceptance incentives still shape behavior: frog and pig both have very low acceptance thresholds, making them relatively likely to take any proposal that gives them even modest positive surplus. Dolphin is more search-oriented and somewhat more selective, so dolphin is likely to test ambitious offers first, especially offering cheap red or blue for valuable green. The key trading logic is that frog can profitably buy red from either opponent, while dolphin can profitably sell red and buy green. Pig is flexible and may accept offers that are weak for others but positive for him. Since proposals go dolphin, frog, pig in every round and holdings update immediately, early successful trades can deplete the best red inventory and shift later proposals toward blue-green exchanges or more modest red deals. A plausible full-game path is: dolphin opens by trying to swap cheap red for green and succeeds because frog finds the deal acceptable; frog then buys additional red from dolphin by paying blue; pig, seeing dolphin still has red and blue surpluses, tries to convert blue into green with frog. In later rounds dolphin keeps monetizing red into green, frog continues accumulating red when profitable, and pig opportunistically arbitrages between frog's demand for red and dolphin's weak valuations. Overall, the likely game contains several accepted trades, mostly driven by dolphin selling red and frog buying it, with pig entering when a proposal is straightforwardly positive and easy to evaluate.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start all players hold 10 of each chip. Dolphin has the strongest incentive to propose a self-favoring red-for-green trade because red is nearly worthless to dolphin and green is valuable. Frog and pig both have low acceptance thresholds, but frog is the more likely accepter for a moderate red-for-green offer because frog values red highly and may be willing to pay green. After seeing whether dolphin can place red, frog is likely to make a direct attempt to buy more red, probably offering blue rather than green because blue is no better than green for frog. Pig then reacts to the new inventory situation with a simpler trade that converts his less-preferred blue into green if someone will take it.

By round 1, dolphin has already improved her position in green and blue while reducing red. Frog has accumulated some red and still values additional red the most, but now also holds less blue and green than at the start, so future offers may become slightly smaller. Pig has shown willingness to do a simple favorable trade. Dolphin likely continues using red as the main bargaining chip, now targeting pig because pig is also willing to pay green for red if the exchange rate is favorable. Frog then keeps pressing for red acquisition, and pig may attempt a more ambitious red-buying offer from frog, though frog's very high red value makes acceptance less likely.

In the final round, players can no longer wait for future opportunities, so positive trades become easier to complete. Dolphin still prefers to unload any remaining red for green. Frog still values red far above other chips and should remain the natural counterparty when the price is not too steep. Pig, with lower search and low acceptance threshold, is likely to end with a straightforward take-it-if-profitable proposal rather than a complicated holdout. Because red inventories have already shifted toward frog, the last round may feature smaller red trades and one opportunistic green-blue exchange.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This game has a strong underlying gains-from-trade structure centered on red chips. Owl values RED most at $1.00, squid also values RED highly at $0.90, while iguana values RED only at $0.30 and BLUE only at $0.20. GREEN is fixed at $0.50 for everyone, so green can serve as a common-value payment chip, while BLUE is especially attractive to owl ($0.60) and moderately attractive to squid ($0.50) but relatively unattractive to iguana. As an analyst, the efficient prediction is that iguana will often want to give up RED or BLUE in return for GREEN, since green is worth more to iguana than either private-color chip. Owl most wants to acquire RED, but because owl does not know others' values during play, owl must infer what terms might be accepted. Squid also wants RED, but can profitably sell GREEN or BLUE for it. The main strategic uncertainty is informational: players only know their own values and must guess whether others benefit. Persona priors suggest owl and squid are relatively deal-ready acceptors, while iguana is more disciplined and somewhat conciliatory but not reckless. That makes modest, clearly profitable trades more likely than highly extractive offers. A likely full-game pattern is early probing, followed by accepted trades in which owl or squid acquires RED from iguana, and occasional competition between owl and squid to accept an offer that is favorable to both.

rounds.round_explanation: In the opening round, all players begin with symmetric inventories, so proposals are driven mainly by private valuations and general bargaining style rather than inventory pressure. Iguana is likely to make a simple calculator-positive offer exchanging a low-valued private chip for GREEN, because green is safely valuable and common. Owl, as a low-search and low-threshold accepter with a very high RED valuation, is likely to propose for RED soon if given the chance. Squid also values RED highly and may accept a profitable owl proposal even if it slightly worsens squid's future bargaining position.

After round 0, one accepted owl trade has shifted some RED away from a non-owl player and increased owl's GREEN outflow. Public holdings now matter more. Iguana still has a strong incentive to convert low-valued RED or BLUE into GREEN, and may now see that at least one counterpart is willing to trade. Owl, if still short of RED relative to preferences, should continue seeking it, but inventory constraints make GREEN scarcer as a payment chip if owl was the realized trader. Squid may instead try to turn GREEN into RED from iguana, since that is profitable for squid and attractive to iguana as well.

In the final round, there is no future strategic value to holding out, so calculator-positive trades become especially attractive. Iguana should still be the player most willing to part with RED for GREEN, because RED remains relatively low value to iguana. Owl and squid both continue to like RED, but owl's extremely high RED valuation makes owl the most likely to post a final aggressive bid if inventory allows. Final-round uncertainty mainly comes from whether a proposer chooses terms generous enough to secure one acceptance rather than chasing a larger surplus and getting declined.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This game has a clear underlying efficient direction, but the players do not know that directly during play. Gorilla values RED most highly at $0.80 and BLUE moderately at $0.50; llama values BLUE most highly at $0.80 and RED at $0.60; whale values RED and GREEN at $0.60 and BLUE at only $0.30. Since GREEN is worth $0.50 to everyone, it mainly serves as a neutral trading medium rather than a source of comparative advantage. The strongest surplus-generating pattern is that gorilla should try to acquire RED and part with BLUE or GREEN, llama should try to acquire BLUE and part with RED or GREEN, and whale is the natural source of BLUE because whale values BLUE least. But each player only knows their own values, so proposals must be based on guesses about what others might accept. That creates uncertainty about counterpart preferences and about whether a good-looking offer for one recipient might also attract multiple acceptances. Persona priors suggest moderate friction but not extreme holdout behavior: gorilla is somewhat cautious and likely to prefer structured, not overly aggressive deals; llama is relatively deal-ready, less guarded, and may accept small positive gains; whale is especially acceptance-prone and conciliatory. As a result, the likely path is that early turns include somewhat cautious or exploratory offers, then trades converge toward simple, calculator-positive exchanges involving BLUE for RED or GREEN. Because accepted trades immediately change inventories and because proposers cannot target which accepter gets the trade if multiple players accept, proposals are likely to be framed so that only one counterpart finds them attractive. Overall, the forecast is for a modest number of accepted trades, especially with whale as a willing counterparty, and for later rounds to reflect inventory adaptation rather than pure surplus maximization.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start all players hold 10 of each chip, so nobody is inventory-constrained. Gorilla moves first and, given a low-aggressiveness and moderately cautious profile, is likely to test a simple favorable trade rather than an extreme demand. Gorilla privately wants more RED, but others may not value GREEN or BLUE enough for a very tough exchange. Llama then has incentives to seek BLUE, likely using RED as payment because RED is not llama's top chip. Whale values BLUE least and is relatively willing to accept positive deals, so whale is a likely early recipient if llama offers BLUE-for-RED or RED-for-BLUE terms that are attractive from whale's side. Whale's own proposal is likely conciliatory and easy to accept, perhaps offering BLUE away in return for RED or GREEN.

After round 0, whale has converted some low-value BLUE into RED and has also swapped away GREEN for more RED; gorilla has gained a bit of RED and lost some GREEN; llama has gained BLUE by paying RED. Public holdings now provide clues even though values remain private. Gorilla may infer that at least one counterpart is willing to part with RED only at nontrivial terms, so a direct attempt to buy RED with BLUE at a one-for-one rate becomes more plausible if gorilla believes BLUE might be attractive to someone. Llama, having already improved by increasing BLUE, may continue seeking BLUE if possible, but must adapt to whale now holding less BLUE. Whale, as proposer, is still the player most naturally positioned to offer BLUE in exchange for something valued more highly.

By the final round, inventories have diverged enough to shape offers strongly. Gorilla now has accumulated extra RED and has given up some BLUE and GREEN; llama has increased BLUE but reduced RED; whale has reduced BLUE and increased RED. Because this is the last round, there is less reason to experiment and more reason to post trades that are immediately calculator-positive. Gorilla no longer needs to chase RED as aggressively and may try to rebalance by using surplus RED or GREEN for BLUE if beneficial. Llama still likes BLUE most and is relatively acceptance-prone, so llama may continue making direct blue-seeking offers. Whale's final proposal is likely modest and acceptable if it gives away remaining low-value BLUE for GREEN, which whale values more than BLUE.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This game is likely to revolve around red-for-blue exchange because green is worth the same $0.50 to everyone, while red and blue create heterogeneous gains from trade. Duck values both RED and BLUE at $1.00, so duck is relatively indifferent between those two colors and can act as a flexible intermediary, though duck should dislike giving away either at a discount. Lion values RED at $0.90 and BLUE at $0.50, so lion strongly prefers acquiring RED and is relatively willing to part with BLUE. Tiger values RED at $1.00 and BLUE at only $0.30, so tiger also strongly prefers acquiring RED and is especially willing to give up BLUE. That means both lion and tiger are natural demanders of RED, while duck is the player most able to trade RED away without a strong private-value penalty relative to BLUE. The main uncertainty is informational: players do not know others' values, only that values lie in the public ranges. So proposals are likely to test whether counterparts will accept seemingly favorable swaps under private calculators. Because acceptances are simultaneous and the proposer cannot target a specific accepter if multiple players accept, proposals that could appeal to both opponents create execution uncertainty. Persona priors suggest duck will make relatively straightforward, disciplined offers and may accept modest gains; lion will be somewhat selective and guarded, less willing to take thin deals; tiger should also reject weak offers but may accept clear positive-value trades quickly. Overall, the most plausible path is a few early probing offers, then one or more accepted red-for-blue trades in ratios favorable to the red holder, with little reason for anyone to trade green except as a low-stakes test.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start, all three players hold 10 of each color, so inventory constraints are loose and the main task is price discovery. Since private values are unknown to others, early proposals are likely to probe exchange rates between RED and BLUE. Duck, as the least value-differentiated between red and blue, is the most natural first mover to test a trade. Lion and tiger both likely prefer to conserve their own RED while trying to obtain more RED from others, so they may reject any request for RED unless compensated generously.

After round 0, lion has already improved by converting BLUE into RED, and duck has also profitably converted some RED into BLUE. Tiger now holds more RED and less BLUE than initially, which may make tiger less eager to keep paying high blue prices for more RED. Public trade history reveals that red-for-blue exchanges are getting accepted, so proposers may continue along that dimension but try to improve terms. Because lion and tiger both value RED more than BLUE, requests that take RED from them are still likely to fail unless the compensation is strong.

By the final round, players have observed enough history to infer that duck is the main feasible source of RED and that BLUE is the main payment medium. Duck has repeatedly benefited from exchanging RED for more BLUE at favorable rates, while lion and tiger have each shown willingness to sacrifice BLUE for RED when the calculator shows a positive gain. Final-round behavior is likely to become slightly more opportunistic: duck may try one more profitable red-for-blue trade, lion may test whether someone will part with RED cheaply, and tiger may either seek one last red gain or stop if terms are too demanding.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This game should revolve around blue chips as the main source of asymmetric value. Bear values BLUE at $1.00 and RED at only $0.20, so bear has the strongest incentive to turn low-value red into blue whenever possible. Goose values RED and BLUE equally at $0.80, making goose the most flexible trader and the easiest likely accepter of modestly favorable offers. Ram values BLUE at $0.80 and RED at $0.50, so ram still prefers blue to red but is willing to part with some blue if compensated enough in red or green. GREEN is common value at $0.50 for everyone, so it functions as a neutral balancing chip rather than a source of private comparative advantage. Because each player only knows their own values during play, proposals are made under uncertainty about counterpart incentives. Still, low acceptance thresholds across all three personas imply many individually profitable offers will be accepted, especially by goose and ram. The main uncertainty is not whether profitable offers are accepted, but whether proposers target the right color exchange rate and whether both potential recipients might accept, creating random matching. Bear is likely to open with blue-seeking trades using red as payment; goose is likely to make conciliatory, easy-to-accept offers; ram is likely to make well-calibrated offers and track inventory carefully. A plausible full-game path is that bear successfully converts some red into blue, goose then turns some blue into red because red is just as valuable to goose but more valuable to others, and ram later uses green or red to fine-tune holdings. Since there are only 3 rounds and no counteroffers within turns, players have reason to accept clear positive-surplus trades rather than hold out aggressively.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start everyone holds 10 of each chip, so inventory constraints are loose and the best opportunities come from exploiting private comparative advantage. Bear should already want more blue and care little about giving up red. Goose and ram are both likely to accept any offer that gives them a positive calculator gain, with goose especially permissive. Early accepted trades are likely to be simple and moderate rather than extreme, because proposers do not know others' exact values.

After round 0, bear has already improved by swapping some low-value red away and may try for another blue-acquisition trade. Goose remains flexible and can profit from taking red from bear or ram at the right price. Ram, being more state-disciplined, is likely to notice which counterpart now has excess red or reduced blue and make a cleaner offer than in the first round. Midgame trades should become slightly more tailored to updated holdings.

In the final round, players no longer benefit from waiting, so individually positive offers become especially attractive. Bear should still prefer blue and may be willing to spend green now if red inventories are lower. Goose may continue making conciliatory balancing trades because equal red/blue values make many exchanges acceptable. Ram is likely to use the last move to secure a modest sure gain rather than gamble on a demanding proposal.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This game should revolve around private-value arbitrage in BLUE and, to a lesser extent, RED, with GREEN acting as a publicly known benchmark chip worth $0.50 to everyone. The strongest structural opportunity is that ram-purple-2824 values BLUE at $1.00, lion-blue-472 values BLUE at $0.60, and octopus-green-4361 values BLUE at only $0.10. That makes octopus the natural low-value seller of BLUE and ram the natural high-value buyer, while lion can also benefit from acquiring BLUE at terms better than $0.60 per chip. RED creates a smaller wedge: lion values RED at $0.50, ram at $0.30, and octopus at $0.20, so octopus is also the lowest-value holder of RED and lion the most likely buyer. Because players only know their own values during play, they cannot directly target the socially efficient counterpart; instead they infer willingness from accepted and rejected offers. The UI surplus calculator makes outright loss-making accepts unlikely, so most accepted trades should give the recipient nonnegative private surplus. Personas suggest all three are fairly disciplined and not especially rejection-prone, so proposals are likely exploratory but still payoff-aware rather than random. A likely pattern is early probing with GREEN-for-BLUE offers, where octopus is willing to part with BLUE cheaply and either lion or ram may accept depending on terms. Competition between two possible recipients adds uncertainty: if a proposal is attractive to both non-senders, the realized match is random. Because inventories update immediately, accepted early BLUE trades can reduce octopus's cheap BLUE stock and make later trades pivot toward RED or toward smaller, more conservative exchanges. Overall, the likely game features a few accepted, individually profitable trades, mostly exploiting octopus's low BLUE valuation and lion's relatively high RED valuation, with some declined probes when proposers ask for too much relative to what recipients privately value.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start, everyone holds 10 of each chip, so inventory is unconstrained and players can test the market. Lion is likely to probe for BLUE because BLUE is their highest private-value margin over GREEN/RED, but lion does not know ram values BLUE even more and octopus values it much less. Octopus has the clearest hidden incentive to sell BLUE for almost anything near public parity, while ram is highly motivated to acquire BLUE but may prefer to wait for favorable terms. Early round 0 proposals are therefore likely to reveal whether BLUE can be bought using GREEN as the medium of exchange.

After round 0, the public history likely suggests that BLUE-for-GREEN trades can clear, especially when octopus is the sender. Lion has already improved their BLUE position and may now look for RED, which lion values as much as GREEN and more than both other players do. Octopus, having already sold some BLUE, still has incentive to continue if the terms are favorable, while ram may become more selective because he has already acquired valuable BLUE and may not want to give it up under any remotely balanced rate.

By the final round, players have more information from observed acceptances and updated holdings. Octopus has repeatedly demonstrated willingness to exchange away BLUE for GREEN, while lion has shown demand for both BLUE and RED. Final-round behavior is likely to be a mix of one more efficient trade attempt and one or two ambitious proposals that fail because the implied private price is too demanding. With no future rounds left, there is less reason to hold back, but the surplus calculator still disciplines accepts.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This game has a clear underlying gains-from-trade structure from an analyst perspective: buffalo-purple-5232 values RED most highly and BLUE least, duck-green-2458 values BLUE most highly and RED only moderately, and cat-yellow-2069 is comparatively moderate on both RED and BLUE while valuing GREEN at the common value. That means the most efficient bilateral direction is usually buffalo sending BLUE for RED, or duck sending RED for BLUE, because each can give away a relatively low-value private chip and receive a high-value one. Cat is the natural middle player because RED and BLUE are both worth more to cat than buffalo's BLUE but less than buffalo's RED or duck's BLUE, so cat can often accept deals that are modestly profitable even when the proposer is trying to keep a large surplus. The key uncertainty during play is that players do not know each other's exact values, so they must infer acceptance from behavior and from the broad public ranges. Because all responders privately see their own exact surplus, proposals that are outright loss-making will be rejected, but a sender may still test ambitious exchange rates, especially early. Persona priors suggest buffalo is relatively organized and deal-ready, likely to make feasible offers and accept small gains; duck is especially acceptance-prone and numerate, making him a likely recipient for marginally positive offers; cat is somewhat more selective and lower-search, so likely to avoid complicated experimentation and accept only clearly positive trades. Across three rounds, this should create some early probing, then convergence toward simple two-color swaps that give the responder a visible gain while preserving the proposer's own surplus.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start, all players hold 10 of each chip, so inventory constraints are loose and the main task is price discovery. Because players only know their own values, early proposals are likely to test whether others will accept favorable rates. Buffalo has the strongest reason to try turning low-value BLUE into high-value RED, but may not know that duck is the best partner for such a trade. Cat's middle valuations make cat more likely to propose a balanced-looking trade for RED. Duck, who values BLUE very highly and is relatively deal-ready, is likely to ask for BLUE while offering RED, a direction that can be attractive to buffalo.

After round 0, public history reveals that cat was willing to trade BLUE away for RED and that buffalo accepted RED-for-BLUE from duck, making the buffalo-duck complement more visible behaviorally even if private values remain unknown. Holdings now matter more: buffalo has extra BLUE and RED, cat has extra BLUE but less RED, and duck has extra RED but less BLUE. This setup encourages buffalo to press again for more RED using BLUE, while cat may find fewer profitable opportunities because cat's inventory shifted away from the chip buffalo most wants. Duck can continue making straightforward RED-for-BLUE requests, which are likely to be accepted by buffalo if terms remain favorable.

By the final round, players have observed repeated successful RED-BLUE exchanges involving buffalo and duck, so bargaining uncertainty is reduced even though private values are still hidden. Inventories are now more uneven: buffalo has accumulated substantial RED and shed BLUE, while duck has gained BLUE and shed RED. That makes further buffalo-duck trade still possible but somewhat tighter on quantities and terms. Cat remains the least naturally matched player because GREEN has no private-value advantage for anyone and cat's red-blue spread is modest, so cat may either make a conservative offer or fail to find an attractive exchange. Final-round behavior should become more opportunistic and concrete, with buffalo and duck trying to extract one more favorable trade before the game ends.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This game has a clear but only partially visible gains-from-trade structure. Butterfly values GREEN most and dislikes BLUE, with RED only moderately useful; Kangaroo values RED highly and BLUE somewhat, while GREEN is fixed middle value; Squid values BLUE very highly, values RED modestly, and values GREEN at the common value. The main efficient cycle is therefore RED flowing toward Kangaroo, BLUE flowing toward Squid, and GREEN often serving as a neutral medium because it has the same known value for everyone. The strategic complication is that players do not know one another's private values during play, so they must infer willingness from acceptances and rejections. Because accept/decline choices are calculator-assisted, recipients should almost never knowingly accept negative-surplus trades. That means proposals are likely to be either exploratory offers that may fail, or conservative positive-surplus offers that exploit uncertainty about who values what. Butterfly's persona suggests low acceptance threshold and orderly state tracking, so she is likely to make relatively deal-ready offers and accept modest gains. Kangaroo is more search-oriented and somewhat more aggressive, so she may test exchange rates that are good for her before settling into cleaner positive-sum trades. Squid is disciplined and patient, with moderate selectivity, so she is likely to reject clearly bad deals but accept favorable BLUE-acquiring opportunities. Since all start with 10 of each chip, inventory constraints are loose early and become relevant only after several accepted trades. A likely full-game path is early probing, then some convergence toward trades involving BLUE for RED or GREEN once behavior reveals likely demand patterns.

rounds.round_explanation: In the first round, nobody has behavioral evidence yet beyond the public chip ranges and their own values. Proposals are therefore likely to be tests. Butterfly is a plausible early proposer of a modest GREEN-for-BLUE style trade because she personally values GREEN highly and BLUE very little, even though she cannot know that Squid values BLUE extremely highly. Kangaroo then has incentives to seek RED, her best chip, possibly by offering GREEN, which is less valuable to her than RED. Squid, moving last in the round, can condition on what was accepted or rejected and may begin targeting BLUE accumulation by offering away GREEN or some RED.

By round 1, the history gives some signal. Butterfly has seen Kangaroo seek RED and Squid seek BLUE. Squid has already successfully acquired RED and then BLUE through positive-surplus trades, suggesting disciplined targeting rather than random exchange. Butterfly is likely to keep offering BLUE away for chips she values more. Kangaroo, after successfully obtaining RED once, may push a little harder for favorable terms, but the calculator should constrain acceptances. Squid, seeing revealed willingness from Butterfly to part with BLUE, is likely to continue trying to obtain BLUE using GREEN, which is equally valued by all and often serves as the cleanest acceptable payment.

In the final round, the revealed pattern is strong: Butterfly is a reliable source of BLUE, Kangaroo strongly wants RED, and Squid values BLUE enough to give up GREEN for it. Endgame incentives can cut two ways: players may either lock in easy positive trades or try one last ambitious proposal. Butterfly's low acceptance threshold and dislike of BLUE make another practical BLUE-for-GREEN or BLUE-for-RED offer likely. Kangaroo may continue to pursue RED, but if inventories and counterpart willingness no longer support a clean gain for others, her offer may fail. Squid, now with more BLUE than before, has less reason to keep buying it at aggressive rates and may instead make a conservative exchange or no successful trade if terms are too demanding.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This game likely revolves around mutually beneficial red-for-blue exchange, with green mostly serving as a low-information but weak bargaining chip. Bat values BLUE most ($0.60) and RED least ($0.20), so bat strongly wants to give up red to obtain blue. Fox values both BLUE ($1.00) and RED ($0.60) relatively highly, but values blue far more, so fox is generally reluctant to give blue away and instead prefers to acquire red using green when possible. Frog values RED most ($0.70) and BLUE least ($0.30), so frog wants to trade away blue to obtain red. From an analyst perspective, the obvious surplus-generating match is bat giving RED for frog's BLUE: bat gains because blue is much better than red, and frog gains because red is much better than blue. Fox can also profit by offering GREEN for RED to bat, because fox values red above green while bat values green above red, but fox's strongest private asset is blue and fox is unlikely to part with it cheaply. The main uncertainty is that players do not know each others' values, so proposals must be based on plausible acceptance rather than known surplus. Given the personas, all three are fairly deal-ready with very low acceptance thresholds, so positive-value offers are likely to be accepted, but proposers may still test slightly self-favoring terms early. Because holdings update after each trade, early bat-frog red/blue exchanges can deplete the best trading inventories and later turns may shift toward green-for-red cleanup trades involving fox.

rounds.round_explanation: In the opening round, players are likely to probe which color pair is tradable without yet knowing others' private values. Bat has the clearest incentive to propose a red-for-blue trade, since that is highly profitable for bat and plausibly attractive to at least one counterpart. Fox is likely to avoid selling blue and instead try to use green to buy red. Frog, seeing whatever happened earlier in the round, is likely to continue pursuing red with blue if inventory allows, because that is frog's strongest direction of gain.

After round 0, bat has already improved by swapping out red and may still have enough red left to continue trading with frog, though inventories are tighter. Fox has likely observed that bat is willing to release red and may repeat a green-for-red structure because it is safe and positive for fox. Frog remains the natural demander of red and may continue to offer blue, but bat's remaining red stock is shrinking, creating more uncertainty about whether another direct red-for-blue exchange can occur.

By the final round, the best bilateral arbitrage has mostly been exploited. Bat has little incentive to give away remaining blue or green, and any remaining red is scarcer. Fox may still search for a small green-for-red improvement if someone has red left, but successful trades become less likely as compatible inventories narrow. Frog, if still short of red, may test one last blue-for-red offer, but the terms may need to be generous enough to overcome bat's reduced red holdings and fox's strong preference for keeping blue.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This game has a strong structural asymmetry: dog-green-8079 and turtle-red-9966 have identical values and both value BLUE most highly ($1.00), while parrot-pink-5400 values RED slightly more ($0.60) and BLUE much less ($0.30). GREEN is fixed at $0.50 for everyone, so the most efficient bargains move BLUE away from parrot toward dog or turtle, and move RED toward parrot from dog or turtle. Because players only know their own values, dog and turtle cannot be sure parrot undervalues BLUE, but the on-screen surplus calculator will make favorable offers easy for each recipient to identify. The main bargaining tension is that dog and turtle compete for the same attractive source of BLUE, while parrot has little reason to accept bad rates because he can usually trade BLUE away profitably. Personas suggest dog and parrot are relatively deal-ready and permissive in acceptance, while turtle is somewhat more selective. A likely path is that dog quickly tests a simple RED-for-BLUE trade, parrot accepts if profitable, and later parrot proposes for RED because RED is his best private color. Once parrot's BLUE inventory shrinks, later offers become smaller or target GREEN as a neutral balancing chip. Uncertainty remains because proposers do not know counterpart values exactly and multiple acceptors are possible, but given these valuations, accepted trades are most likely to be bilateral exchanges between parrot and one of the BLUE-lovers, with dog somewhat more active and concessionary than turtle.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start everyone has 10 of each chip, so inventory constraints are loose and first-round behavior is mostly about discovering exchange rates. Dog has a strong incentive to ask for BLUE and can profitably pay in RED or GREEN, but RED is the more natural offer because it is less valuable than BLUE to dog and plausibly attractive to someone else. Parrot should be receptive to giving up BLUE if the compensation is in RED. Turtle also wants BLUE but moves after seeing two earlier turns, so turtle may either imitate a successful pattern or avoid proposing a deal that parrot no longer needs.

By round 1, the public history points toward a workable pattern: parrot has accepted and initiated RED-for-BLUE exchanges, revealing some willingness to part with BLUE for the right price. Dog likely continues to pursue BLUE, perhaps with slightly better-targeted terms. Parrot, seeing that both others are plausible counterparties, can keep converting BLUE into RED or possibly into GREEN if inventories make that easier. Turtle has already gained some BLUE, so turtle may either push for a little more or become more conservative if earlier proposals were rejected.

In the last round, players have less reason to hold out for future opportunities, so marginally positive deals become more likely to clear. Public holdings now likely show parrot with reduced BLUE and increased RED, while dog and turtle have accumulated some BLUE. Dog may still try one more favorable BLUE purchase, but parrot can reject if the rate is too thin. Parrot then has a final chance to exploit remaining demand for RED or to rebalance toward GREEN if a sure positive trade is available. Turtle, moving last, may test a small deal, but with limited remaining counterpart need and no future rounds, the most likely finish is one accepted parrot-centered trade and one final rejected exploratory offer.

parsed_target.game_explanation: All three players value GREEN at $0.50, so green acts like a common-value currency and is unlikely to be willingly traded away at a discount. BLUE is only worth $0.10 to everyone, making it the least valuable chip and the natural thing to give up. RED creates the main informational asymmetry: badger-green-4722 and peacock-purple-5231 each value RED at $0.30, while unicorn-blue-7096 values RED at $0.50. That means unicorn is the likely net demander of RED, while badger and peacock are the likely suppliers of RED if they can receive enough GREEN or, less attractively, enough BLUE. Because players only know their own values during play, badger and peacock cannot be certain that unicorn values RED more highly, but their offers can test this. The calculator makes accept/reject decisions payoff-transparent on each side, so offers that are strictly negative for recipients should almost always be declined. Persona priors suggest badger is relatively deal-ready and low-search, so likely to make simple, salient offers early; peacock is disciplined and conciliatory, so likely to construct feasible, positive-surplus trades carefully; unicorn has a low acceptance threshold and low guardedness, so is the most likely to accept modestly favorable deals quickly. The most likely game path is therefore: early probing offers involving RED for GREEN or BLUE, several declines when terms are too demanding, and at least one successful trade where unicorn gives GREEN to obtain RED from badger or peacock. Holdings then constrain later turns, but because everyone starts with large inventories, feasible trade space remains wide across all three rounds.

rounds.round_explanation: In the opening round, players are still learning from sparse signals. Badger, who is more anchor-sensitive and deal-ready, is likely to post a straightforward ask that tests whether anyone will part with GREEN for RED. Peacock is likely to react selectively because GREEN is valuable and RED is not especially valuable to him. Unicorn is the only player for whom RED is worth as much as GREEN, so unicorn is the most plausible accepter of a balanced RED-for-GREEN exchange. Once that pattern is revealed publicly, later proposals can target unicorn more directly.

By round 1, the public history suggests unicorn is willing to swap GREEN for RED at roughly parity, while RED-for-BLUE offers are too weak to clear. That should push badger toward a cleaner 1-for-1 RED-for-GREEN proposal. Peacock, with strong bookkeeping and a more conciliatory style, is also likely to target unicorn with similar terms. Unicorn may continue experimenting with cheap BLUE offers when proposing, because BLUE is low value to everyone and unicorn benefits strongly from obtaining RED, but these offers remain unattractive to badger and peacock.

In the final round, there is little reason for badger or peacock to accept low-value BLUE for RED, and both now have public evidence that unicorn is the best counterpart for RED-for-GREEN trades. Endgame pressure can make proposers a bit more direct, but the calculator still disciplines outcomes toward individually profitable trades. A plausible finish is one more successful RED-for-GREEN exchange from badger, a somewhat greedier but unsuccessful ask from peacock, and one last failed attempt by unicorn to turn BLUE into RED.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This profile should produce fairly active but imperfect bargaining rather than total gridlock. The key value structure is that dog-orange-6603 and owl-purple-3001 both value BLUE highly at $1.00, while dog also values RED highly at $0.80 and owl values RED only at $0.30. Mouse-green-4201 is the natural low-value holder for RED at $0.20 and values BLUE moderately at $0.60. GREEN is a flat $0.50 for everyone, so it mainly serves as a neutral medium of exchange rather than a source of private gains from trade. The most important hidden complementarities are therefore mouse wanting to shed RED to dog, owl wanting to shed RED to dog, and mouse or owl being willing to send BLUE to dog only if they can get enough of something they personally like in return. However, because players do not know each other's values, they must infer willingness from observed acceptances and inventory changes. The personas suggest all three are relatively deal-ready and not especially rejection-prone, so proposals are likely to aim for modest positive surplus rather than extreme holdout demands. Dog is especially likely to make straightforward, calculator-friendly offers and accept small gains quickly. Mouse is reasonably disciplined and not very guarded, so he is likely to test practical exchanges involving RED. Owl is the strongest bookkeeper and may adapt best to revealed inventory conditions. Even so, three-player competition creates uncertainty: a sender cannot target a specific recipient, and if both non-senders accept a favorable offer one is chosen at random. That randomness pushes proposers toward offers that are attractive to only one likely counterparty, especially when values differ sharply by color. A plausible full game therefore features an early failed broad offer, then a couple of selective accepted trades where mouse and owl unload low-value RED to dog for GREEN, followed by later inventory-sensitive attempts to obtain BLUE from dog that mostly fail because dog values BLUE too much to part with it on terms others would accept.

rounds.round_explanation: In the opening round, nobody has any trade-history evidence yet, so proposals are most likely to be simple tests based on the sender's own values and generic beliefs about what others might accept. Because GREEN is common value, it is a natural numeraire for exploratory trades. Dog should already know that giving up RED is costly but may still test whether others will overpay in GREEN for it. Mouse then has the clearest private motive to move low-value RED out. Owl, with good bookkeeping discipline, is likely to infer from any accepted trade that dog may be a willing buyer of RED-for-GREEN deals.

By round 1, dog has visibly accumulated RED and spent some GREEN, while mouse and owl have each shown willingness to exchange RED for GREEN. This creates a stronger public hint that dog is a buyer of RED and that GREEN can clear trades. Dog may now try to leverage his remaining GREEN to get even more RED cheaply, but other players have less surplus inventory urgency than before. Mouse, having improved his mix away from low-value RED, may switch attention toward BLUE, which he values more than RED. Owl may also look for BLUE or further GREEN, but any attempt to buy BLUE from dog is difficult because dog privately values BLUE at the ceiling.

In the final round, players have learned that RED-for-GREEN can work with dog as the buyer, while BLUE is hard to pry away on moderate terms. End-game pressure can make offers slightly more concessionary, especially from dog given her deal-ready profile, but inventories now matter: dog has less GREEN remaining than at the start, and mouse and owl each have already reduced some RED. A realistic ending is one more accepted RED-for-GREEN exchange if the terms are still individually profitable for dog, while more ambitious BLUE trades remain unattractive.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This game likely centers on red chips. Green is worth the same $0.50 to everyone, so green serves as a neutral trading currency. Red is worth most to rabbit-yellow-6681 ($1.00), somewhat to alligator-orange-6315 ($0.60), and least to buffalo-purple-4069 ($0.50). Blue is worth most to alligator-orange-6315 ($0.50), then rabbit-yellow-6681 ($0.30), and least to buffalo-purple-4069 ($0.20). That creates the clearest gains from trade in two directions: buffalo-purple-4069 is relatively willing to give up blue and red, while alligator-orange-6315 especially values blue and rabbit-yellow-6681 especially values red. Since players only know their own values, proposals are made under uncertainty about others' acceptance thresholds, but the UI lets each responder see whether a trade is personally profitable. That tends to eliminate strictly bad acceptances. The two deal-ready, low-guarded players, alligator-orange-6315 and rabbit-yellow-6681, are likely to accept modestly favorable offers, while buffalo-purple-4069 is somewhat more moderate and may make fairer, easier-to-accept proposals. Because accepted trades update holdings immediately and there are only three rounds, early proposals may test exchange rates, then later proposals become more targeted as players infer from acceptances which chip directions are attractive. A plausible path is that some ambitious opening offers fail, followed by accepted green-for-red or blue-for-red exchanges, especially involving buffalo-purple-4069 as a source of lower-valued blue or red and rabbit-yellow-6681 as a demander of red.

rounds.round_explanation: In the first round, players have full inventories and little behavioral information beyond the raw chip environment. Early offers are likely to test whether others will part with red or blue at favorable rates. Because responders can see their own net surplus exactly, overly aggressive offers should be declined. Alligator-orange-6315 may start by trying to turn red into blue, which is personally attractive but likely unattractive to the others. Buffalo-purple-4069, with relatively low blue value and a conciliatory style, is the most likely early proposer to make an actually acceptable trade. Rabbit-yellow-6681 strongly values red and may probe for red-acquiring trades or hold out for later opportunities.

By round 1, one thing is publicly clear from behavior even if values remain private: alligator-orange-6315 was willing to accept blue in exchange for green, suggesting blue may matter to him, and buffalo-purple-4069 was willing to part with blue for neutral chips. Rabbit-yellow-6681's failed red request suggests that asking cheaply for red may not work. Proposals now likely become more grounded in revealed willingness to trade. Alligator-orange-6315 may try to monetize his now-larger blue inventory or acquire neutral green. Buffalo-purple-4069 can continue making relatively balanced offers. Rabbit-yellow-6681, being exploratory and red-focused, is likely to keep seeking red but may need to pay with more green to get acceptance.

In the final round, players can use the public history to infer some directional preferences. Buffalo-purple-4069 has now twice shown willingness to exchange red or blue against green, which makes buffalo-purple-4069 the natural counterparty for rabbit-yellow-6681's red demand. Rabbit-yellow-6681 has finally succeeded by paying generously for red, so alligator-orange-6315 may now test whether rabbit-yellow-6681 will also pay with blue or green for more red. Endgame pressure can support more acceptance of modest positive-surplus deals, but because responders still have exact private payoff calculators, trades that are zero or negative for them should still be declined.
